nameMeanings.name: Braose
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Eckhard, Gerhard, Manfred, Otto.
from a vernacular pet form of the personal name Ambrosius (see Ambrose ).

variants.name: Ambrose
meanings: English and Scottish: from the personal name Ambrose (French Ambroise, Latin Ambrosius, from Greek ambrosios ‘immortal, divine’), which was popular throughout Christendom in medieval Europe. Its popularity was due in part to the fame of Saint Ambrose (c. 340–397), one of the four Latin Fathers of the Church, the teacher of Saint Augustine. In North America, the English form of the surname has absorbed cognates from other languages, e.g. Czech, Slovenian, and Croatian Ambrož (see Ambroz ), and also their derivatives, e.g. the Slovenian patronymic Ambrožič.
Irish: from Mac Ambróis ‘son of Ambrose’ (see 1 above); a West Munster name, which has also been Anglicized as McCambridge .

nameMeanings.name: William
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Probably the most successful of all the Old French names of Germanic origin that were introduced to England by the Normans. It is derived from Germanic wil ‘will, desire’ + helm ‘helmet, protection’. The fact that it was borne by the Conqueror himself does not seem to have inhibited its favour with the ‘conquered’ population: in the first century after the Conquest it was the commonest male name of all, and not only among the Normans. In the later Middle Ages it was overtaken by John , but continued to run second to that name until the 20th century, when the picture became more fragmented.

nameMeanings.name: de
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.

historicEvents.title: Battle of Hastings
historicEvents.description: Battle of Hastings was fought between the Norman-French army and the battle-wary English army on October 14, 1066. Duke William of Normandy believed he was the rightful king rather than Harold, who had been crowned king several months earlier. Harold was killed in battle and William was crowned king on Christmas Day 1066.

historicEvents.title: Domesday Book
historicEvents.description: Domesday Book is Britain's earliest extant public record with information containing land surveys and landholdings commissioned by William I. The book was completed in 1086.

historicEvents.title: The Crusades
historicEvents.description: A series of religious wars between Christians and Muslims, known as the Crusades, occurred between 1096 and 1291, and played a large role in European Christian dominance in the Middle East.

en.lifeSummary: When William de Braose was born in 1049, in Briouze, Orne, Lower Normandy, France, his father, Robert de Braose, was 49 and his mother, Gunnora de Centreville, was 37. He had at least 1 son with Agnes de St. Clare. He died after 1095, in Bramber, Sussex, England, United Kingdom.

ko.lifeSummary: William de Braose(이)가 1049년 프랑스 배세-노먼디 른 브리오우즈에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Robert de Braose(은)는 49세였고, 그의 어머니 Gunnora de Centreville(은)는 37세였다. 그는 Agnes de St. Clare(와)과 적어도 한 명의 아들을 두었다. 그는 1095년 이후 영국 잉글랜드 수세스 브램버에서 사망했다.

ru.lifeSummary: Когда William de Braose родился в 1049 г. в местности бриоуз, орн, бассе-норманди, Франция, его отцу по имени Robert de Braose было 49 лет, а матери, которую звали Gunnora de Centreville, 37. У него был по крайней мере 1 сын от Agnes de St. Clare. Он умер после 1095 г. в местности брэмбир, суссекс, Англия, Великобритания.
